briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. I will now begin the climate briefing for March 7, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis: Earth Exceeding Safe Limits
sections.content: Recent research findings clearly indicate an acceleration of global warming. According to domestic researchers, the rate of global warming has sharply accelerated since 2015 [3, 33], and KAIST has warned that current carbon emissions are double the 'planetary boundary,' Earth's safe limit [2]. Notably, 'compound disasters,' where heatwaves and droughts occur simultaneously, have surged eightfold domestically. This represents a new form of compound disaster spreading globally, analyzed as a phenomenon that has rapidly increased since the 2000s [4, 5, 21, 39]. Furthermore, ecosystem disruptions are intensifying, such as spring arriving earlier due to climate change [25].

sections.title: ⚠️ Impacts of Climate Change on Communities and Ecosystems
sections.content: The deepening climate crisis poses a direct threat to communities and ecosystems worldwide. In the U.S., lobster catches in Maine have once again declined due to high costs and the impacts of climate change, affecting the local economy [22, 29, 32], and fishermen in Greenland are also facing threats to their livelihoods due to climate change [31]. Youth in California directly appealed to the Court of Appeal regarding the impact of climate change on their lives, representing the concerns of future generations [24]. Furthermore, British experts have warned that food stockpiles must be strengthened in preparation for climate shocks and war, indicating that climate change can also significantly impact national security [38].

sections.title: 🌱 Expansion of Domestic Carbon Neutrality Efforts and Policies
sections.content: Amidst this crisis, various efforts to achieve carbon neutrality are expanding domestically. Namseoul University and Cheonan City have launched a joint educational program [6], and cooperation among various entities is active, with Geoje City, Nonghyup Geoje City Branch, Korea Atomic Energy Research Institute, and the Korea Chemical Industry Association signing carbon neutrality MOUs [7, 9]. Icheon City is recruiting complexes to participate in the eco-friendly living apartment certification evaluation [8], while Yuseong-gu and Yangju City are encouraging participation in the Carbon Neutrality Point System, thereby spreading a virtuous cycle of energy [10, 11, 12]. Furthermore, it is emphasized that the core objective of inter-Korean forest cooperation also extends beyond ecosystem restoration to climate change response [1], demonstrating an all-encompassing commitment to carbon neutrality.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Current Challenges
sections.content: Domestic efforts to transition to renewable energy are accelerating further. The Ministry of Climate is providing unstinting policy support, including promoting the designation of 'Renewable Energy Day' as a legal holiday and holding discussions to expand solar power dissemination [18, 19]. Taean County has attracted approximately 1 trillion KRW in offshore wind power investment from Singapore [17], Namwon City is expanding its new and renewable energy projects [14], and Yeonggwang County has declared a vision of an 'Energy Basic Income City,' making renewable energy a right for its citizens [16], demonstrating active movements within local communities. Notably, the case where companies voluntarily increased rooftop solar panel installations after regulations were transformed into 'consideration' shows a positive change [20]; however, challenges remain, such as calls for a fact-finding investigation into uncompleted solar power generation projects [15].

briefing.closing: As we have seen in today's briefing, the climate crisis is having a severe impact across all aspects of our lives. At the same time, various domestic and international efforts to overcome this crisis are ongoing, offering us hope while also urging more proactive action. This concludes the daily climate briefing for March 7, 2026.

articles.original_title: 2000년대 들어 폭염-가뭄 ‘복합재해’ 급증···대기와 지면 서로 데우는 ‘악순환’
articles.description: Domestic research indicates a rapid increase in compound disasters, where heatwaves and droughts occur consecutively, since the early 2000s. An example is the dried-up bottom of Obong Reservoir in Seongsan-myeon, Gangneung-si, Gangwon-do, observed on August 31 last year. Analysis suggests that heatwaves, which previously ended as simple hot weather, now lead to severe droughts that desiccate the land, thereby strengthening a 'vicious cycle' that escalates into powerful compound disasters. On the 7th, Kim Yong-jun of Hanyang University's Ocean Convergence...

summary.phenomenon: Since the early 2000s, Korea has seen a rapid increase in compound disasters involving consecutive heatwaves and droughts. An example is the dried-up bottom of Obong Reservoir in Seongsan-myeon, Gangneung-si, Gangwon-do, observed on August 31 last year.
summary.cause: Heatwaves, which previously ended as simple hot weather, now lead to severe droughts that desiccate the land, thereby strengthening a 'vicious cycle' that escalates into compound disasters.
summary.outlook: The 'vicious cycle' escalating into powerful compound disasters is strengthening.

articles.original_description: 지난해 8월31일 강원도 강릉시 성산면 오봉저수지 바닥이 말라 있다. 정효진 기자2000년대 초반 이후 폭염과 가뭄이 연달아 발생하는 복합재해가 급증했다는 국내 연구 결과가 나왔다. 과거에는 단순한 더위로 끝났던 폭염이 이제는 땅을 바짝 말리는 가뭄으로 이어지며 강력한 복합재해로 번지는 ‘악순환의 고리’가 강화되고 있다는 분석이다.7일 김용준 한양대 해양융···

articles.original_title: UK must stockpile food in readiness for climate shocks or war, expert warns
articles.description: Prof Tim Lang says country produces far less food than it needs to feed population and is particularly vulnerable The British government should be stockpiling food, according to a leading expert on food policy, as it is not prepared for climate shocks or wars that could cause the population to starve.Prof Tim Lang of City St George’s, University of London said the UK produced far less food than it needed to feed itself, and as a small island that relied on a few large companies to feed its giant

summary.phenomenon: The UK produces significantly less food than needed to feed its population and is particularly vulnerable, being unprepared for climate shocks or wars.
summary.cause: The UK's food production is insufficient and vulnerable because it is a small island nation relying on a few large corporations for food supply.
summary.outlook: An expert has warned that the UK government should stockpile food, as the population could face starvation in the event of climate shocks or wars.
